Ok, so the lines of code you need to look at are here: https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre/blob/master/sceptre/diffing/stack_differ.py#L257

Basically, the way the diffing works _right now_ is:
* If a given parameter is defined on the stack config, diff it's value with the deployed config
* If a given parameter is NOT defined on the stack config and the deployed parameter value is identical to its default value, delete the parameter from the deployed config, since we're relying on the default value so there should be no diff detected.

However, the change I think you'll need to make is to add a new condition to that:
* If a parameter is defined on the stack config as `None` and the parameter has a default value, delete that parameter off BOTH the generated config and the deployed config.

Regarding this test, then, I think part of your problem is that you're saying that the default value on the CloudFormation stack for the "new" parameter is `None`, but that's not a valid default value. What you need to test is:

First, you should update the expected_generated_config and expected_deployed_config properties so that the parameters are deepcopied as well. That is important. 

```python
        # The default value for the "new" parameter is "default_value"
        self.deployed_parameter_defaults["new"] = "default_value"
        # It just so happens that the deployed parameter value IS the default value, 
        # meaning we didn't override it
        self.deployed_parameters["new"] = "default value"
        # On the Stack Config, we're setting the value to None, which means we're effectively 
        # NOT setting any value at all.
        self.parameters_on_stack_config["new"] = None
        # We need to modify the expected generated config to be diffed. Look at the property 
        # for more details on how it's created to start with
        expected_generated_config = self.expected_generated_config
        expected_deployed_config = self.expected_deployed_config
        # We should expect that there is no "new" parameter specified at all in the generated_config or the 
        # deployed_config        
        del expected_generated_config.parameters['new']
        del expected_deployed_config.parameters['new']

        self.differ.diff(self.actions)
        self.command_capturer.compare_stack_configurations.assert_called_with(
            self.expected_deployed_config, self.expected_generated_config
        )
```

You'll also need test cases for when:
* There is no default value on the parameter
* There is no deployed config
* When the parameter value on the deployed config is NOT the default value but the parameter on the generated config is None (in which case it defaults to the deployed config).

> as far as I can understand from the `invalid_lockfile_behavior` help it is basically telling pants how to deal with issues in the lock file when trying to read/resolve it...

As Eric said: Pants actively consumes the header to decide whether a lockfile is _stale_ ... not just when it is broken/invalid. So stripping the header and/or ignoring a broken header would essentially mean that users would need to manually remember to update lockfiles after requirements/settings edits.

Disabling the header would also mean not being able to take advantage of #15704 (when it is implemented), so the entire lockfile would always be re-written, even after adding a single dep, for example.

Let's remove this validation here (and the new validator function).

We've made a conscious decision to avoid validating any of the user's provided rendering params across our SDK, since the only mechanism we currently have for writing and maintaining these validations would involve a massively manual and brittle engineering effort. For example: what happens if tomorrow we change the painter to accept `q=max` or something? Updating the validator in one library would be fairly easy, but updating the validators across every library would take a lot of time and effort, and in the meantime our users would be left out in the cold waiting to use the new painter feature.

Note that we do have plans to fix this stuff, in the future. There's some work scheduled soon to add Typescript output to [`imgix-url-params`](https://github.com/imgix/imgix-url-params), which would allow us to add this level of parameter validation to all of our JavaScript libraries while keeping the maintenance burden down. I'm sure we'll learn a lot through the course of that project, and once it's done we can figure out what it would take to provide similar single-source-of-truth validators for our non-JS libraries as well.

I think your instinct to add a validator here is *absolutely* the correct one, but since we've consciously decided to punt on validating `params` in this library for the moment (but not forever), I think adding a single validator for a single parameter here will probably sow more confusion than help.

This is subtle since it touches on how `asyncio` works in a pretty deep way:

`asyncio` has an ambient event loop (`asyncio.get_event_loop()` returns it) that is always running. `pulumi-language-python-exec` (our bootstrap script that loads a user's Pulumi program) initializes this event loop on startup and shuts it down when the program exits.

Unlike e.g. JavaScript, Python coroutines (`async def` functions) do nothing when called except return a coroutine object. In order to actually execute the function, it has to be scheduled on an event loop, which is what `asyncio.ensure_future` does here. `asyncio.ensure_future`, when given a coroutine as an argument, submits the coroutine for execution on the ambient event loop and returns a future that is completed whenever the coroutine completes.

So what we do here is call `asyncio.ensure_future` to schedule to coroutine, returning a future, and then we immediately `await` the future to produce a value (`future_return`).

Yes, the "fixed" format can be a bit confusing. Each line of a file has 80 characters divided into ten blocks of 8 characters.

- short format: item (cell, coordinates) per line, max 10 fields, 8chars per field 

  example: quad cell - key, cell_id, cell group, ND1, ND2, ND3, ND4
  ```
  CQUAD4  18      1       20      21      30      29
  ```

- long format: item = at least two lines, the last field (8 chars) in line is a "continuation tag", which is equal to the first field in the following line

  example: hexahedron
  line1: key, cell id, cell group (empty), ND1, ND2, ND3, ND4, ND4, ND5, ND6, unique continuation tag
  line2: continuation tag, ND7, ND8
  ```
  CHEXA   1000            1331    449     241     341     159     123     +13e8   
  +13e8   122     141
  ```

Coordinates - long format means 2 * 8chars per coordinate, two lines:
line1: key (`GRID*` = long grid), id, node group (empty), X, Y
line2: `*` = continuation, Z
```
GRID*   45                                  6.000000E-02    3.000000E-02
*           0.000000E+000
```               

Short format:
key, id, node group (empty), X=0.003456, Y=0.001234, Z=0.005678
```
GRID    45      0.0034560.0012340.005678
```

If I `pip install prettytable[windows]` on non-Windows, will it make a difference?

The `init()` is only done for Windows OS.

https://github.com/tartley/colorama#initialisation says:

> Applications should initialise Colorama using ... `init()`
>
> On Windows, calling init() will filter ANSI escape sequences out of any text sent to `stdout` or `stderr`, and replace them with equivalent Win32 calls.
> 
> On other platforms, calling `init()` has no effect (unless you request other optional functionality; see "Init Keyword Args", below). By design, this permits applications to call init() unconditionally on all platforms, after which ANSI output should just work.
> 
> To stop using Colorama before your program exits, simply call `deinit()`. This will restore `stdout` and `stderr` to their original values, so that Colorama is disabled. To resume using Colorama again, call `reinit()`; it is cheaper than calling `init()` again (but does the same thing).

Some thoughts:

We could just call `init()` for all operating systems.

Or better, put it in a `try`/`except ImportError` for in case colorama isn't installed.

And in fact, we only want to call `init()` when using colour themes. If we're using plain mono, we don't want any extra import overhead.

So maybe only attempt the import/`init()` inside `set_theme`?

We could also do it when `theme != None`, and call `deinit()` when `set_theme(None)`.

(Maybe also guard the import/`init` with a `platform.system() == "Windows"`?)

A solution to @smac89's problem could done with `.dockerignore`. They probably have something like this:
in project source:
```
$ ls -a
setup.py
Pipfile
Pipfile.lock
src/<many-python-files-here>
.venv
Dockerfile
```

Now, they (naively) do something in `Dockerfile`
```
ADD  .  /usr/src/app
WORKDIR  /usr/src/app
RUN pipenv install

@bramkragten I took another look, I still think there is no need to explicitly 'do a round of `_renderEmptySortable` when this._uncheckedItems is changed'.

Because at [line 150](https://github.com/home-assistant/frontend/pull/7296/files#diff-3f9c900506d8e374961fa5be5db01f33e7d214a7e3f02ecd9e0dcb825fc3b993R150): `guard([this._uncheckedItems, this._renderEmptySortable]...` decides that when either one of the 2 values (`_uncheckedItems`, `_renderEmptySortable`) changes, the content of sortable `div` will be re-rendered ([doc](https://lit-html.polymer-project.org/guide/template-reference#guard)). 

When add an item while sorting the list, `_uncheckedItems` is changed therefore the content of sortable `div` will be re-rendered. Unless you are talking about the scenario that: the user adds an item after the user starts dragging and BEFORE the user puts it down, but I don't see how that is possible.
Also when storing the new order failed, `_uncheckedItems` is changed therefore the content of sortable `div` will be re-rendered.

In short: the list is messed up only after drag-n-drop, we do a round of `_renderEmptySortable` to re-render the list (also clean up the zombie elements). In any other cases, the change of `this._uncheckedItems` will always re-render list, no need to use `_renderEmptySortable`, also there is no zombie elements generated by drag-n-drop.

Two (at least) trains of thought on where to write the logs.

1. well-known location
1. user's working directory

Guiding the choice is the answer to *Who needs this logging?*  Initially, the developer who is expects a predictable location.  Longer term, the user needs a record of what they have typed and in what sequence.  Writing the logs to the user directory means they can copy that directory (which may contain other content such as *.py files with their custom plans).

If the developer can tolerate logs going into the user directory, that should be the default.  **But**, the location should be configurable.

Another configurable should be 

* un/limited number of logs
* un/limited length of one log file

I like this suggestion. If we do make a naming change here, we should consider updating the other languages as well to be consistent.

Another option:

- `GetConfigValueAsync` (singular) for a single `ConfigValue`.
- `GetConfigValuesAsync` (plural) for the config map.

Which is similar to the built-in [`Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.environment.getenvironmentvariable?view=net-5.0) and [`Environment.GetEnvironmentVariables`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.environment.getenvironmentvariables?view=net-5.0).

Another option: for languages that support overloads (C#, TypeScript, Python), we could have two overloads with the same name (e.g. `GetConfigAsync`). The overload that accepts just the `stackName` returns all config, the overload that accepts both the `stackName` and `key` returns just the value:

```c#
Task<ImmutableDictionary<string, ConfigValue>> GetConfigAsync(string stackName);
Task<ConfigValue> GetConfigAsync(string stackName, string key);
```

Go doesn't support overloads, so if we move to this approach, we'd need to either keep Go's naming as-is, or rename based on the other naming suggestions above. Or another possible option for Go is `GetConfig` (single value) and `GetConfigMap` (all config).

Current draft spec states that TTL should never be longer than the lease time, but not a must:

```
   TTLs sent in SRP Updates are advisory: they indicate the SRP client's
   guess as to what a good TTL would be.  SRP servers may override these
   TTLs.  SRP servers SHOULD ensure that TTLs are reasonable: neither
   too long nor too short.  The TTL should never be longer than the
   lease time (Section 4.1).  Shorter TTLs will result in more frequent
   data refreshes; this increases latency on the DNS-SD client side,
   increases load on any caching resolvers and on the authoritative
   server, and also increases network load, which may be an issue for
   constrained networks.  Longer TTLs will increase the likelihood that
   data in caches will be stale.  TTL minimums and maximums SHOULD be
   configurable by the operator of the SRP server.
```

It's broadcasting; part of this general facility:

```python
>>> ak.Array([10, 20, 30]) + ak.Array([[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]])
<Array [[11, 12, 13], [], [34, 35]] type='3 * var * int64'>
```

It applies across the board for all functions of more than one array, so it would be a little strange if concatenation was an exception. Also, using `broadcast_and_apply` will cause axes above the relevant one to be broadcasted, so it's doubly strange for the relevant axis to not be broadcasted. This is just a consistency thing (and likely convenient, since physicists often have arrays of particle parameters and arrays of event parameters, which are at different levels of depth).

this could run into issues if the first four bytes are already constrained to 1 solution, or are effectively concrete, for example if the API is used to execute a function with symbolic arguments

```
contract.func(m.make_symbolic_value())
```

will result in a tx data that is an ArrayProxy (and thus `issymbolic()`), even thought the first four bytes are really concrete. if we add contradicting constraints on the first four bytes (such as a function preconstrain), that will result in an infeasible state, and solver errors raised later.

we can semi-reliably detect if the func has is already constrained by checking if there can be more than 1 solution. something like

```
# ask for 2 solutions. if we only get one back, there can only be 1.
hash_not_constrained = len(state.solve_n(data[:4], 2)) > 1
if hash_not_constrained:
   # hash not constrained, it's safe to do the preconstrain stuff
```

>So you won't be able to rerun a previous cell ran with runcell? I think that would seem a bit limiting to our users...

This is unfortunate but I think this would be a 'ah ok' moment for most users and not a deal breaker, since users can't run a cell from the console right now. Cell based work is mostly all in the editor anyway (change code -> hotkey) it seems unlikely that a user would return to the console after making a change to a cell in the editor.

I guess the ideal interface is call `runcell('my_cell', 'my_file')` and have `runcell` execute the right code cell in the right file. But code cells without names or the same name end that idea. 

Another interface is just call `runcell()` in the console and the IPython kernel asks the editor for the code in the cell that the cursor is in, but I believe this isn't currently possible with qtconsole and `runcell()` still might not do what users expect

All these things considered it seems code cells are best run from the code cell itself and not the console. This mirrors the notebook experience.


>So for that to work (from the UX point of view) we probably need to avoid saving runcell in IPython's history.

I agree, it certainly shouldn't be in the "up button" for previously used commands.  It's not useful there at all.  I'll see what I can do, but the difficulty of changing this command history was what lead me to make `runcell`.

My original goal of all this was to have a filter on the history to not include code executed from code cells and `runfile` ( see https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/issues/7233) but there was no way to differentiate code cells from anything else because they where just copied code just like anything else.  I wanted this because pressing up and down through long blocks of code was pretty error prone and the history log wasn't overly useful.

At this point it only has a name and a file as the arguments because I thought it would be good have an indication to the user of what code it was running currently. Having this function call in the history log might help the user see what code was run at that point to give context to the surrounding commands

So: we can't have errors raised in the `self.opaque` initialization

1. metrics that can be reported at most once per session (e.g. `commandRun`) - in this case, the stack will always be of length one.

One wrinkle: AFAIK, each call to `socket.recv()` allocates a new `bytes` object that is large enough for the entire given chunksize. If large allocations are more expensive, passing a too-large buffer is probably bad for performance. (The allocators I know of use 128KB as their threshold for "this is big, mmap it instead of finding a free chunk" but if one used 64KB instead and we got a mmap/munmap pair on each receive, that feels maybe bad?)

My intuition favors a much lower buffer size, like 4KB or 8KB, but I also do most of my work on systems that are rarely backlogged, so my intuition might well be off when it comes to a high-throughput Trio application.

Another option we could consider: the socket owns a receive buffer (bytearray) which it reuses, calls `recv_into()`, and extracts just the amount actually received into a `bytes` for returning. Downside: spends 64KB (or whatever) per socket in steady state. Counterpoint: the OS-level socket buffers are probably much larger than that (but I don't know how much memory they occupy when the socket isn't backlogged).

For a lot of the experiments we've been doing it doesn't make any sense to try and start from the middle of an epoch, since there's no guarantee that the previous training run used the same datasets as the current one. My plan is to follow up on this PR with another one that makes all training runs start from epoch 0, and make the global step be the "version of a checkpoint", which is both more precise and matches the language used by other researchers. Then we can say "release 0.5.0 was trained on this data for 75000 steps" in releases, and there's no confusion of epoch counts varying wildly when the dataset changes.

This method is used both for reachability, and for `ensure_remote_has_recursive` (although perhaps it never encounters a `Tree` in practice?). Reachability will use this method for any digest held in memory in `pantsd`, which will be `Snapshots`/`ProcessResults`, etc.

Depending on how you have implemented the conversion from `Tree` to `Directory`, it's possible that both `Snapshots` and `ProcessResults` end up holding directory-digests rather than tree-digests, and so a tree will never be reachable there. But Tree is a sortof interesting structure... currently to expand `Snapshots` we recursively expand `Directories` from the database: being able to load an entire Tree in one shot might be a useful performance boost.

Just realized this may cause the parent lost synchronization with the child. I think there are some confusions about when the "CSL IE" should be included.

From 802.15.4-2015 and Thread spec, it seems the header IE is only required for synchronization messages:

> 2 The SSED Child MAY send periodic traffic to the Router in any form including application
3 data messages or MLE messages to maintain synchronized communication with its Parent.
4 These messages MUST include the CSL IE. An SSED Child that changes its CSLPeriod after
5 initial synchronization SHOULD send an immediate IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Data or Command
6 frame containing a CSL IE to the Router.

So not all messages sent out from SSED need to include "CSL IE". From my understanding, if a message expects retransmissions, it should not include "CSL IE", and a retransmission frame can safely keep identical to its original frame.
Thoughts? @brianljt @jwhui

So let me explain why this is harmless instead (having wrong running_size). The worst case scenario here is we would have "lower"  running_size than it is supposed to be. The worst possible issue here is we will kill less idle workers than we needed to for 200 ms (which is fine because eventually idle workers will be killed properly).

The standard library is a bit schizophrenic in how it treats `timeout` values that are supposed to be infinite, but I can't think of a place it uses `inf`. Older APIs use `-1`, while newer APIs use `None` to mean infinite. I think using `None` would be a more expected choice and help avoid mistakes if people transition from in-process locking to multi-process locking.

Basically this allows us to cast inputs to the op that were not specified by the user (e.g. you make a convolution via symbolic API, you don't need to pass weights and biases to it, MXNet will generate them). So what we do here is create a symbol using the original function (which will create all the other children), then take those children and cast them. MXNet (nor NNVM) does not allow, however, to manipulate a symbol children after it was created. So what we do is we create a new, atomic symbol (atomic means it does not have any children set yet, but has all the params the same as the symbol used to create it), and populate that symbol with casted inputs.

*Before* this change, AuthController removes the item from LocalStorage, but then `resetRenewalTimer` schedules a call to `this.renew()` with a delay of 0ms, so we immediately renew and reset the value in LocalStorage.

I think we need to distinguish *two* expiration times: expiration of the TC credentials (15 minutes) and expiration of the access_token (might be a lot longer).  If on page load the access_token is still valid but the TC credentials are not, then we need to renew the TC credentials but need not delete the user.  So it probably makes sense to track both of those expiration times on the user class, using distinct property names.

If the access_token is no longer valid, then we need to try to renew it.  For auth0, there's a [hidden-iframe way to do that](https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster-tools/blob/17302c900904b892bc66f359c683e855beccb9a3/src/auth/auth0.js#L35-L46).  For GitHub, I'm not sure how best to do it -- maybe just signing the user out in that case.

This PR is about TC credentials expiration.  The access_token renewal can be left to a followup.

In 5.8, when `__repr__()` is called, the training time printed out is a string of the form, "1 d 23 h 57 m 32 s". This string value was also stored as a state variable called `_training_time_as_string`. There was also a `training_time` state variable that would store the number of seconds training took. 

As of this PR in its current state, we have a state variable called "training_time" that also stores the number of seconds training took. I think it's definitely not okay to print out the training time as number of seconds instead of the more informational way in which we were printing it out earlier.

Here are the alternatives along with my take on them.

1. Add logic on the C++ side to compute the training time as a string to match what we printed pre-6.0.
2. Use existing Python code (the `_seconds_to_string` function in `src/python/turicreate/toolkits/object_detection/object_detector.py` to create a state variable at the Python layer only for `__repr__` purposes)

I think Option 1 makes the most sense -- we should make a `training_time_seconds_to_string` function that lives in `src/toolkits/utils/` that we can call on the C++ side. @nickjong thoughts?

```python

min -> 1

`buffer` and `round_robin` are two more optional inputs that a call can have. 

`buffer` (int) - same as what you have in consecutive availability, non-negative integer representing the number of minutes of buffer time you want around existing meetings.

`round_robin` (string) - accepts "max-fairness" or "max-availability" (or None) as inputs. "max-availability" will find the union of availability among the given emails/free-busy. "max-fairness" will do the same but it will exclude people who were recently booked as a robin through the events endpoint if applicable.

1. it's unclear if users update these independently.  Or if they just run our scripts to update their `pulumi` install, in which case this would not happen.
2. Presuming that it *does* happen, we have several options available as to what we can do.  Including:
    1. We can error here and require that they update their `pulumi` install.
    2. We can silently fallback to our existing behavior where we call the async codepath and then deasync it.

complicates all the other layers of the stack: you need case conversions
everywhere, and in general need to be careful that your conversions are
locale-insensitive to pass the Turkish test, and properly handle puns
like (U+00B5, U+039C, U+03BC) and (U+03A3, U+03C2, U+03C3), …. It helps
a lot that you restrict the character set to URI-valid characters;

behavior, an encoded form that’s twice as large, etc. I suspect that it

This is one of the things I'd love to eradicate in a future revamp of our test suite. "Not tested yet / no reason to test" situations shouldn't need explicit hand-written expected output, but if we have the test case inputs ready to go, we could definitely auto-generate a snapshot of the current behavior and alert if it ever changes. It would be a nice defense-in-depth strategy, but only worth it if we can make it easier to do.

Right now, if writing a test targeting something that's generally only interesting e.g. for a particular SQL flavor (e.g. `@fold` in MSSQL), we either have to write the expected test outcome for other SQLs and other query languages (tedious!) or `SKIP_TEST` to reduce the amount of effort a PR takes and not take advantage of the additional test opportunity. This is where an automatic snapshot test would be useful: low effort yet still helpful.

> I thought with my changes Netif::SubscribeExternalMulticast registers multicast addresses with one /n/mr package.

I think as long as we subscribe multiple MAs in one `ipmaddr add` command, there should be only one /n/mr message. The `otIp6SubscribeMulticastAddress` API can be kept as is (i.e. subscribe to one MA at a time), but we can call `otIp6SubscribeMulticastAddress` multiple times in one `ipmaddr add` command processing. 

Going into further details, multiple calls to `otIp6SubscribeMulticastAddress` will  trigger multiple `Get<Notifier>().Signal(kEventIp6MulticastSubscribed)`, which will be merged to one `kEventIp6MulticastSubscribed` event, which eventually triggered the `/n/mr` message containing all the MAs.

Let's follow both of the scenarious:
1. Column is physically chunked and we access each chunk's buffer separately:
```python
>>> col.num_chunks()
3
>>> len(col._pyarrow_table.column(0).chunks)
3
>>> ch1, ch2, ch3 = col.get_chunks()
>>> ch1.num_chunks(), ch2.num_chunks(), ch3.num_chunks()
(1, 1, 1)
>>> ch1.get_buffers()["data"][0].ptr, ch2.get_buffers()["data"][0].ptr, ch3.get_buffers()["data"][0].ptr
(140593764278336, 140593764278720, 140593764279168) # Each chunk has its own buffer, so there's 3 different pointers
```
2. Column is physically chunked and we access the chunked column buffer. As there're 3 separate buffers, we have to copy the data of 3 chunks into a single contiguous buffer
```python
>>> col.num_chunks()
3
>>> len(col._pyarrow_table.column(0).chunks)
3
>>> col.get_buffers() # Combines chunks to provide single contiguous buffer
{'data': (Buffer({'bufsize': 104, 'ptr': 139876896911360, 'device': 'CPU'}), (<DTypeKind.INT: 0>, 64, 'l', '=')), 'validity': None, 'offsets': None}
>>> col.num_chunks()
1

Why the state is mutated during the second flow? It's to not to redo the combining every time we access `.get_buffers()` or some other method that require to materialize actual buffers (`.describe_categories`). Redoing combining every time may result into a strang behaviour:
```python
>>> ptr1 = col.get_buffers()["data"][0].ptr # Do chunks combining for the first time
>>> ptr2 = col.get_buffers()["data"][0].ptr # Do chunks combining again
>>> # Although we called exactly the same method, the results are different
>>> ptr1 == ptr2
>>> False
>>> mapping1 = col.describe_categories["mapping"] # Materialization is required, do chunks combining for the first time 
>>> mapping2 = col.describe_categories["mapping"] # Do chunks combining again
>>> # mapping1 != mapping2 in case categoricals are unordered
>>> mapping1 == mapping2
>>> False
```

The idea of using `mOffset` of zero to indicate the type is interesting and smart, but I am not sure if we should go for it since I think it may be limiting. It's correct that in a DNS message the offset should never be zero, but we may use a `Message` as a data blob storage, for example to store metadata about query (we do this actually in the new DNS client) and in that case it can happen that a name is encoded at any offset (maybe at offset zero).

-------

On using `union`, I actually started with that. Basically I had a `mType` and then a `union` but decided against it.

- On the upside, I agree it can reduce the `sizeof(Name)` by few bytes. But I think in all cases, the instances of `Name` would be temporary/local vairable and the size won't matter much.
- On the downside, it adds a code size and computation overhead (since now we need to check `mType` against set of values). 

Not using `union` has the advantage that complier/toolchain can optimize/inline the code better:
- Compliers typically generate very efficient code fo comparing a pointer/value against zero (this can end up being combined into the instruction that loads the value from memory to a register (same instruction does zero comparison as well).
- Most advanced toolchain can optimize simple struct instance when passing them to methods/function and use  registers to pass the fields rather than allocating stack space to generate the object. `union` won't be optimized like this.

ingress function from pandas API (e.g. ``read_csv``) is called, the compiled
query from pandas API is passed to the ``FactoryDispatcher``, which forwards
the query to execution specific IO class. For example, ``PandasOnRayIO`` is the

This IO class defines the Modin frame and query compiler classes to ensure correct
object is constructed. It additionally exposes supported
 ingress functions, which are mix-ins containing a combination of the engine-specific
class for deploying remote tasks, the class for defining how to parse the given
file format (``PandasCSVParser`` for example), and class that handles
file format specific chunking on the head node (see dispatcher classes
implementation :doc:`details </flow/modin/core/io/index>`). This ouput from
the IO class ingress function is formatted into a Modin DataFrame for further processing.

> we should ONLY consider the DCS config to be authoritative.

Yes, in the ideal world we should, but it is not always possible.
This specific feature could be built only by relying on the DCS config cached by Patroni.

> Consider the case where we have a replica which has been added to a cluster, but the initially given local config did not include the static_primary element

Ok, now I see what you don't understand. There is no such "local" config.
Here is what we have:
1. The global config that is stored in DCS (typically in the /config key). Every heart-beat cycle Patroni loads (at least tries) the cluster view by calling https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/fb06af9adbf1267dbc0da72c2d8c3eaf802561a6/patroni/ha.py#L135-L141. As a result, the cluster view is cached in the HA, and in the DCS implementation. The last one could be accessed via the `cluster` property https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/fb06af9adbf1267dbc0da72c2d8c3eaf802561a6/patroni/dcs/__init__.py#L763-L766.
2. There is also a cached config. It is stored in the `$PGDATA/patroni.dynamic.json`. After every heart-beat cycle it could be updated from https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/fb06af9adbf1267dbc0da72c2d8c3eaf802561a6/patroni/__main__.py#L110-L112
3. Last, there is `bootstrap.dcs`, which only works if DCS is empty and there is no `patroni.dynamic.json`.

When Patroni starts, it first loads config from the `patroni.dynamic.json`, and updates it by whatever is stored in DCS. If both are missing, it will use the `bootstrap.dcs` as the last resort: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/fb06af9adbf1267dbc0da72c2d8c3eaf802561a6/patroni/__main__.py#L38-L49

> I do believe that this is probably why Patroni is currently implemented (irrespective of this PR) to abort startup if the DCS can not be contacted.

Exactly, on start we always want to see what is stored in DCS.

> Thoughts?

Coming back to my original comment. The `self.patroni.config` is updated only _after_ the heart-beat loop. The most up-to-date value is available in the `self.cluster.config`. Hence, the `is_static_primary()`  and `is_static_primary_configured()` methods should be implemented similar to the `get_standby_cluster_config()`.

`CoreWorkerProcess::Initialize` can only be called once by workers, but not drivers. A driver may initialize and shutdown Ray multiple times. A typical use case is Python and Java tests. And `CoreWorkerProcess::Shutdown` is meant to be called by driver only. (the documentation is at the top of `class CoreWorkerProcess` declaration.) A worker can shut down the `CoreWorkerProcess` by returning a system exit status in the task execution callback (also documented), and in this way, the `CoreWorkerProcess::RunTaskExecutionLoop` will finish and the worker process is ready to quit.

A good way to avoid this confusion is to put the `ray::Stats::Init` and `ray::Stats::Shutdown` to the constructor and destructor of `CoreWorkerProcess`.

In general, the guidelines are sound. A few things come to mind, however, that I think make them remain guidelines rather than strict rules:
- Tools that do runtime analysis of code (e.g. `monkeytype` and `pyannotate`) will always suggest the most-specific type, since they don't and can't know which of the properties of the object are important (e.g. whether `Sequence` is okay or whether modification is important and it has to be `List`). Between more typing and less typing across the codebase, we should always prefer more, so we should in general make that type-hint-addition process as smooth as possible.
- It's difficult to know in general for existing code whether it is allowed some capability but chooses not to use it (e.g. mutating the list), versus whether it isn't allowed that capability and should only know `Sequence` instead. So when type-hinting existing code, it's not always clear whether `List` or `Sequence` is appropriate, nor is it feasible to always find that out (per the above point).

So while these guidelines are good for newly-designed and newly-written code, and are in the general good spirit of "be liberal in what you accept and strict in what you provide," I think applying them to type-hinting existing code must be done with care and good judgment.

They're pretty much orthogonal! `ready` includes both active and idle workers (only pending is excluded), and `idle` only includes idle workers (both active and pending are excluded), so they're not inverses of each other. A bit more on each of these heuristics...

The 80% cap for the ready/total ratio ensures that, in the worst case, we don't try to create more actors than 125% of the cluster's CPU/GPU resource capacity; this is a basic heuristic for resource-based backpressure. This cap will probably be replaced with the executor's resource management + a heuristic based on the work queue size and misc. profiling (queue arrival rate, actor startup time, task execution time).

The 50% cap for idle/total is a super basic "more than half of the actors are idle" heuristic for when we've created too many actors; this was the simplest heuristic I could come up with that bootstraps, scales, and degrades pretty gracefully:
1. At operator startup, pending actors aren't penalized, so we're able to start an initial wave of actors in parallel.
2. The pool's steady-state should be > half of the actors are active with < half idle, so we should have solid load-balancing + an idle pool ready for input queue bursts. With load-balancing based on tasks-in-flight, this is probably a bit conservative, actually; we could look at decreasing this to 0.25 on some benchmarks to see if we really need that much pool slack.
3. As a pool becomes temporarily less active, e.g. there's a delay bubble in upstream processing, we won't downscale.

I'm guessing that we might keep this heuristic around as a super conservative idle cap, since it's a simple alternative to the common solution of idle timeouts and can be complementary (a fallback) to downscaling triggered by the executor control loop based on its resource management.

Should be fun to experiment with tweaking these going forward!

If you have a more difficult control flow (e.g. multiple `if`s or `for` loops) in a list comprehension, which spans multiple lines, it should be refactored. That does not mean not to use list comprehension, it just means you need more steps.

When in doubt, i suggest you write out both options and choose the one that is more readable. Readability > anything else (unless performance is a priority).

In this case we have
```python
failed_intervals = []
for result in result_data:
    intervals_for_result = _get_failed_intervals(result)
    if intervals_for_result != []:
        failed_intervals.append({"identifier": result["identifier"], "failed_intervals": intervals_for_result})

return failed_intervals
```
versus

```python
        return [
            {"identifier": result["identifier"], "failed_intervals": _get_failed_intervals(result)}
            for result in result_data
            if _get_failed_intervals(result)
        ]
```

You can see that the first one has more lines of code, requires more variable names. And it tells you *how* to build the result.
The second one tells you more about *what* is being returned. In general that is a bit favored for readability. But IMO large list comprehensions suffer from the same problem, where we just effectively move all the control flow inside a list comprehension. In that case it is probably better to do it in multiple steps.

```python
    failed_intervals = ((result["identifier"], _get_failed_intervals(result)) for result in result_data)
    return [
        {"identifier": ident, "failed_intervals": intervals} for ident, intervals in failed_intervals if intervals != []
    ]
```

Here I tried to separate two steps. The first one calculates failed intervals for all the results. The second one filters and repackages them.

But in the end a lot of this is subjective.

Contents are allowed to be longer than the nodes that contain them (i.e. have unreachable elements) because of how they're sometimes created: making several arrays is not an atomic operation, but if appends are performed from the innermost to the outermost, allowing long contents to be valid means that the whole array is valid for each of the atomic steps.

An example is if ArrayBuilder is filling some interior content with `begin_list` or `begin_record` that hasn't yet been paired with an `end_list` or `end_record`. During that time, the array as a whole is valid; it just doesn't have the partial list or record, though you might see some of the partial contents if you investigate its `content` during this time.

    try_path('/run/lock') or
    # Finally, fall back on the work dir and hope it's a legit filesystem.
    cls.getToilWorkDir(config_work_dir)
)

if source:

Defining a function-local static variable that leaks like this is a common pattern (recommended by Google C++ style guide, for example: https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Static_and_Global_Variables).  Since the destructor would only be called when the program exits, the "leak" is irrelevant as long as it is just memory.  You can define it in a slightly more complicated way (https://github.com/google/tensorstore/blob/master/tensorstore/internal/no_destructor.h) to avoid the extra heap allocation, but one extra heap allocation is presumably not very important.

There are actually two different cases to consider here:

- For `get_local_internals` used within an extension module, Python never actually unloads extension modules (because in general it is not really feasible to do that safely), so the destructor is never invoked anyway.
- The only case where the destructor previously was getting called was in the case where `get_local_internals` is used outside of an extension module, e.g. when using an embedded interpreter, or when statically linking other libraries directly to the Python interpreter itself (as we do internally at Google).

Looking more closely at `finalize_interpreter`, though, the use of `get_local_internals` seems suspect:

https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/424ac4fe1bde46894a56775e78a702199a03137b/include/pybind11/embed.h#L229

By default there will be a separate copy of `local_internals` for each extension module, and for the embedding program itself.  Therefore, this will only free types and exception handlers that were registered module-local by whichever module calls `finalize_interpreter`.  Any types registered by other modules will remain registered.  To work properly, this logic would need to change.  Though given all of the bugs and limitations with multiple initialization and finalization of the python interpreter, I think it might be better to just not support that at all.

> Also, why is an actor class being inherited if "**module**", "**qualname**" and "**init**" are in args[-1]?

This is a pattern that I noticed. When I inherited a class, then there were 3 arguments, ("<DerivedClassName>", "<ModuleOfTheDerivedClass>",  "<A dict having module, qualname and init>"). If you notice the signature of `ActorClass.__init__`, then it matches with these 3 arguments. However, when you simply call `cls(<arguments>)` (as done in #21868), then the above pattern is not observed which means the `ActorClass` is not inherited. I know this is somewhat hackish, but let me know if Python has any other technique to figure if some class can be inherited or not.

The other approaches I tried included, checking `DerivedActorClass.__subclasses__` but that doesn't work. I am not sure if there is any other way to figure out if the user is inheriting an actor class or simply calling the `__init__` method from a `classmethod`.

If this loop can run more than once, and without abort being called, then the task will be appended to `_blocked_tasks` multiple times.

This avoids the bug and makes it clear what things aren't changing on each iteration:
```python
async def acquire(self):
    if self._held:
        task = trio.lowlevel.current_task()
        self._blocked_tasks.append(task)
        def abort_fn(_):
            self._blocked_tasks.remove(task)
            return trio.lowlevel.Abort.SUCCEEDED
        # may need multiple attempts since reschedule() is not instant
        while self._held:
            await trio.lowlevel.wait_task_rescheduled(abort_fn)
    self._held = True
```

The whole topic of advanced indexing is deeply confusing, and I've heard people say that it's too advanced to be understandable (and useful).
Your second example, though, is deprecated and will change in the future (to be aware of here as well!). If wrapping the whole index in a sequence, a `tuple` must be used, not a `list`. Here's the warning from current Numpy:

    FutureWarning: Using a non-tuple sequence for multidimensional indexing is
    deprecated; use `arr[tuple(seq)]` instead of `arr[seq]`. In the future this will be
    interpreted as an array index, `arr[np.array(seq)]`, which will result either in an
    error or a different result.

Re: compatibility with Numpy: within the reach of the current unit tests, I think advanced indexing is 100 % compatible with Numpy's. So I guess we can just point to the Numpy documentation for a detailed explanation.
The only remaining difference is with respect to strided views, where Numpy has more cases (in basic indexing) where a view is returned. Getting on par there would require a much bigger effort (extend storage backend).

Since the user specifies a CloudFormation template file path in their cluster launcher config, but this file isn't included in the config file hash used by caching, we could miss updates made to the CloudFormation template unless they disable the config cache. 

Maybe we could also include any CloudFormation template file contents as part of the config file hash in a follow-up PR?

The other alternative would be to allow the CloudFormation template file to be inlined as part of the cluster launcher config, but I'm not particularly inclined to take this path due to (1) a lot of copy-pasting (and manual errors) from existing CloudFormation templates into cluster launcher configs, (2) a lot of cluster launcher configs becoming mainly comprised of CloudFormation template config (making it harder to distinguish Ray config from CloudFormation config), and (3) encouraging a bad design pattern for similar integrations (e.g. CDK) to just continue trying to flatten large external file dependencies in the Cluster Launcher Config.

The problem is that the decorator is annotated with

```py
def log_api_usage_once(fn: Callable) -> Callable:
```

This means `mypy` assumes `Callable` as full annotation for the decorated function. This is roughly equivalent to

```py
def foo(*args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any

If we restrict the decorator for the functional transforms for now, we can fix this by annotating with `Callable[..., torch.Tensor]`.

```python
def __call__(self, instances, num_epochs, shuffle):
    for epoch in num_epochs:  # handle num_epochs == None here
        for instance in instances:
            yield instance.as_tensor_dict()
```

Per our spec: https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler#constraints-and-rules

```When filtering (via @filter) or type coercion (via e.g. ... on Animal) are applied at or within a vertex field marked @optional, the @optional is given precedence:

- If a given result set cannot produce a value for the optional vertex field, it is preserved: the @optional directive is applied first, and no filtering or type coercion can happen.
- If a given result set is able to produce a value for the optional vertex field, the @optional does not apply, and that value is then checked against the filtering or type coercion. These subsequent operations may then cause the result set to be discarded if it does not match.```

So we may not pretend that the edge does not exist.

@jfuss apologies for the slow reply, I was on vacation. 

So I had a look at the SAM CLI code to see how `DockerContext` and `Dockerfile` metadata are used by the SAM CLI.

I found 4 other usages. 

Just one usage is relevant to this PR: 

In [this code block](https://github.com/i18n-tribe/aws-sam-cli/blob/bdb8b961bdcb237105a2776b09cde9944a1f7e5d/samcli/lib/build/app_builder.py#L384-L423) in `samcli/lib/build/app_builder.py` 

We read the metadata properties:

```python
dockerfile = cast(str, metadata.get("Dockerfile"))
docker_context = cast(str, metadata.get("DockerContext"))
```

Then we make `docker_context` into an absolute path:

```python
docker_context_dir = pathlib.Path(self._base_dir, docker_context).resolve()
```

Then we call `docker build` using the python docker client, passing the properties as arguments:

```python
build_args = {
    "path": str(docker_context_dir),
    "dockerfile": dockerfile,
}
build_logs = self._docker_client.api.build(**build_args)
```

So in summary - 

- `Dockerfile` is passed without modification to `_docker_client.api.build` as the `dockerfile` argument
- `DockerContext` is converted to an absolute path, and passed to  `_docker_client.api.build` as the `path` argument

Here is the documentation for ['_docker_client.api.build'](https://docker-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#module-docker.api.build)

And those arguments are:

```
path (str) – Path to the directory containing the Dockerfile
dockerfile (str) – path within the build context to the Dockerfile
```

In my opinion, the documentation for `path` is a little bit ambiguous.

Does this mean that `path` is:

1. the immediate parent directory containing the Dockerfile? 
2. a directory that ultimately contains the dockerfile, which might be nested in child folder(s)?

I would completely understand if something thinks it means `1.` - this is what I thought at first.

But if we look at the documentation for `dockerfile` - it says `path within the build context to the Dockerfile`

Because `dockerfile` can be a `path within the build context` - it can be a path (potentially via subfolders) to the `Dockerfile`.

So in fact, `2.` is the correct interpretation.

This interpretation is reinforced by the `docker cli` documentation for [docker build](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/build/)

Usage is:

```
docker build [OPTIONS] PATH 
```

Where `PATH` will be `DockerContext` in the SAM CLI.

And the option `--file` will be `Dockerfile` in the SAM CLI.

In the `docker cli` documentation, they refer to something called the `build context`.

The `build context` is very similar to `PATH` / `DockerContext`.

The `build context` is a copy of the folder specified in `PATH` / `DockerContext`, excluding files specified in `.dockerignore`.

So `--file` / `Dockerfile` is a path to a docker file within the `build context` (usually a relative path is used).

There is no requirement for the docker file to be in the root of the `build context`.

I.E. `--file` / `Dockerfile` can be a path via subfolders to the docker file.

Here's [the documentation for `--file`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/build/#specify-a-dockerfile--f).

The documentation has examples where `--file` is a path via subfolders to the docker file like:

```
docker build --file dockerfiles/Dockerfile.debug .
docker build --file dockerfiles/Dockerfile.prod .
```

In the example above, `PATH` is `.` - the current directory.


And there's a quote in [this section of the docs](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/build/#text-files).

```
By default the docker build command will look for a Dockerfile at the root of the build context. 
The -f, --file, option lets you specify the path to an alternative file to use instead. 
This is useful in cases where the same set of files are used for multiple builds. 
```

The use case `where the same set of files are used for multiple builds` is exactly what this PR is trying to solve.

This is the use case described in the [PR description](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/pull/4074#issue-1313551509).

This use-case is supported by the `AWS CDK`.

This use-case is also - in fact - already supported by the `SAM CLI`.

The [`AWS CDK` docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cdk/api/v2/python/aws_cdk.aws_lambda/DockerImageCode.html#aws_cdk.aws_lambda.DockerImageCode.from_image_asset) support this use-case explicitly:

```
directory (str) – the directory from which the asset must be created.
file (Optional[str]) – Path to the Dockerfile (relative to the directory). Default: ‘Dockerfile’
```

The [`SAM CLI` docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/serverless-application-model/latest/developerguide/serverless-sam-cli-using-build.html#build-container-image) do not support this use-case explicitly:

```python
Dockerfile - The name of the Dockerfile associated with the Lambda function.
DockerContext - The location of the Dockerfile.
```

The `SAM CLI` developer guide sound like interpretation `1.` of the documentation for ['_docker_client.api.build'](https://docker-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#module-docker.api.build).

But in fact, the `SAM CLI` code works perfectly well if `Dockerfile` is a path via subfolders to the docker file.

And I've personally used this 'undocumented feature' in multiple `SAM` projects I have worked on.

In other words -

This PR is removing code that added a limitation to the `SAM CLI`, when the limitation did not previously exist in either `SAM` or `CDK`, but `SAM CLI` docs said this limitation exists.

So I've created a [PR to update the `SAM CLI` developer guide](https://github.com/awsdocs/aws-sam-developer-guide/pull/159), so people will be aware of this capability of the `SAM CLI`.

I've also created a PR to [update the documentation for the Docker Python Client](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/pull/3043) to be more clear.

---

A further note -

Above there is a link do documentation for the `docker cli`. 

The python docker SDK doesn't use the `docker cli`, it uses the `docker api`.

So I checked the docker API documentation for [build](https://docs.docker.com/engine/api/v1.40/#operation/ImageBuild)

It takes an argument:

```
dockerfile - Path within the build context to the Dockerfile.
```

It also takes a tar archive which contains the `build context` - documented as:

```
Build an image from a tar archive with a Dockerfile in it.

The Dockerfile specifies how the image is built from the tar archive.
It is typically in the archive's root, but can be at a different path or have a different name by specifying the dockerfile parameter.
```

I.e. `dockerfile` 'is typically in the archive's root' - but it can be a path to a dockerfile in a subfolder.

And I checked the source code of the docker python sdk, to see how it's interacting with the docker API.

Here's the [code where it creates the `build context tar archive`](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/b7daa52feb8b897fb10fbe82c6e49a273746352a/docker/api/build.py#L150-L163).

This is a copy of the folder specified in `path` / `DockerContext`, excluding files specified in `.dockerignore`.

And [on this line](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/b7daa52feb8b897fb10fbe82c6e49a273746352a/docker/api/build.py#L174) it passes `Dockerfile` to the docker API as `dockerfile`.
 
---

For reference, here are the 3 other usages of `DockerContext` metadata, which are not relevant to this PR:

- In samcli/commands/build/build_context.py - [If it's a docker lambda, confirm these metadata properties are defined](https://github.com/i18n-tribe/aws-sam-cli/blob/c2b947d6087cd88f2174e6a3b3079865dc06394e/samcli/commands/build/build_context.py#L562-L573).
- In samcli/lib/utils/resource_trigger.py - [trigger a code refresh if this metadata is changed the the developer](https://github.com/i18n-tribe/aws-sam-cli/blob/1cda797850907cf28c7b4f80180cba3627360fd4/samcli/lib/utils/resource_trigger.py#L260-L264)
- In samcli/lib/providers/sam_function_provider.py - [converting `DockerContext` to be an absolute path if necessary](https://github.com/i18n-tribe/aws-sam-cli/blob/4b4e6e44ea7dd7e4d9f73001214dc34a5aa59701/samcli/lib/providers/sam_function_provider.py#L413-L422)

The intention isn't that each `otLinkedBuffer` corresponds to a single TCP segment. The TCP implementation is responsible for breaking up the stream into segments, and the boundaries between TCP segments does not necessarily match the boundary between linked buffers. I think that the logic of how to break up a TCP stream into individual segments belongs in the TCP implementation, not in the application using TCP.

See my comment [here](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/6491#discussion_r616770478) for more details on what a truly zero-copy send path might look like.

Here as the doc states, `typing.cast` will do nothing at runtime, it is really only for mypy and help it understand the type when the code cannot let it infer that. Basically here, we know from `__init__` that if `self.acme_version ==1`, then `self.client` is of type `Client`, and `ClientV2` otherwise.

I thought about two other ways to fix the problem here, but each of these approaches was not better in my opinion:
* the most obvious, `if isinstance(self.client, Client)`: it is elegant on mypy side, because alonside with the fact that `self.client is declared` as `Union[Client, ClientV2`, it will properly infer this case (and also my IDE) when `self.client` is actually a `Client` or a `ClientV2`. Sadly tests are not happy because we frequently mock these classes, making the `isinstance` not working as expected. For sure I could use a `spec` on the `MagikMock`, but at that time it was too much code compared to a simple `cast`.
* as an alternative, assign the ACME client to `self.client_v1` or `self.client_v2`, then use `if self.client_v1` or `if self.client_v2`. First, it adds a lot of code to the `BackwardCompatibleClient` juste to make mypy happy. Also, you still need to have two `if`, and not a `if/else` because both `self.client_v1` and `self.client_v2` will be `Optionals[]`. All of this for a class which is fundamentally planned to be removed at some point.

Is that a general method for checking sign change? Based on code it seems to only check when going from positive (data[x] >= 0) to negative (data[x+1] < 0). What about from negative (data[x] <= 0) to positive (data[x+1] > 0)?

Code-wise, unfortunately, Python's `for` loops are rather slow. Since you have data in numpy it is advised to do a high level operation. To check whether there's a zero crossing, likely the best is to check whether there's a negative sign when multiplying consecutive values, e.g.
```python
def sign_change(data):
    prev_values = data[:-1]
    next_values = data[1:]
    counter_idx = (prev_values * next_values) < 0
    return np.sum(counter_idx)
```

Here. `np.sum` works because implicitly `False` and `True` are evaluated to `0` and `1`, respectively.

There's always going to be integer maths issues on these charts unless you're really careful how you size them.

Based on the previous bug report, the key thing is that the scale is consistent with the bar drawing and that the bar overwrites the interval line when it is the interval value.  For example, the 0.5 interval marker should be exactly the same height as a bar of 0.5 value.

I think the key calculation is that your value for each line should be `value = (i + 1) * scale / int_h` rather than the stuff you do with deltas and precision rounding.
